column2: Fully licensed driver (previously a learner driver)

column2: Fully licensed driver (previously a learner driver)
column5: A dedicated 2-in-1 dashcam system (road-facing and cabin-facing cameras)
column7: Four main barriers to using the present the app on daily basis: 1. The hassle of transferring videos from the dashcam to the website (requires two transfers of huge files); 2. The hassle of learning how the website works (even though the author made it as easy as possible); 3. The need to recalibrate the analysis, possibly a few times, before obtaining reliable results. 4. For many people knowing that they are being recorded can be a factor of psychological distraction, introducing nervousness and/or bias in their driving method.
column8: I think a web-app is not the best realisation of the concept. It would make more sense and be easier to use if it was part of a 3-in-1 dashcam system/app. Additional features I would welcome: recognition and analysis of a broader settl of manoeuvres, better suited for learner drivers.
column10: Good luck with your project report!🤞

column2: Fully licensed driver (previously a learner driver)
column5: A dedicated 2-in-1 dashcam system (road-facing and cabin-facing cameras)
column7: Not an issue with the system per se, I would simply be unlikely to watch back footage of my driving to see where mistakes occurred. To me, dashcams are a preventative measure for theft or useful as a form of proof if accidents occur, the additional time that it would take to review footage of driving where there is no accident makes me highly unlikely to use it.
column8: A feedback report that could be sent directly to one's device. Instead of having to parse through the videos, if it highlights the mistakes (or frequent mistakes) and compiles them into a clean place to see, rather than having to go back to the old video, that may make one more likely to look.
column10: Would be interesting to see how well the system works with lower resolution footage from cheap dashcams.
